* It=E2=80=99s
like it never happened: Last year, Cond=C3=A9 Nast decided to take its Midd=

al outfit Nervora ( https://puck.news/gq-china-july-issue-might-get-buried-=

=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), w=
hich published Vogue Arabia under a licensing agreement. Naturally, Nervora=
 fought back in court, claiming that Advance, Cond=C3=A9 Nast=E2=80=99s par=
ent company, did not engage =E2=80=9Cin the good
faith negotiations for renewal of the license mandated by that license agre=
ement, but rather has only engaged in sham discussion while it plotted to u=
ndermine the protections.=E2=80=9D And yet, Cond=C3=A9 Nast proceeded with =
the breakup, earlier this month appointing Manuel Arnaut=E2=80=94that=E2=80=
=99s right, no L=E2=80=94as head of editorial content at Vogue Arabia.=C2=
=A0

Arnaut was named editor-in-chief of Vogue Arabia in 2017, and has worked at=
 various Cond=C3=A9 magazines on the
licensing partner side. =E2=80=9CJoining the parent company has always been=
 a dream of mine,=E2=80=9D he wrote in an Instagram post in early January. =
For someone like Arnaut, who=E2=80=99s been relegated to the B-team for muc=
h of his career, working with Anna Wintour is the ultimate achievement. But=
 not everyone is thrilled about the change, if only because years of work h=
ave been erased from existence: Everything published under the title Vogue =
Arabia before 2025 has been taken
offline. (Actually, all the Cond=C3=A9 brands in the Middle East=E2=80=94Ar=
chitectural Digest, GQ, Cond=C3=A9 Nast Traveler, too=E2=80=94have started =
their websites from scratch.)=C2=A0

My presumption is that Nervora owned and operated the business while simply=
 paying a brand-licensing fee to its American partner, and therefore has no=
 obligation to keep the articles and images online. (I reached out to Nervo=
ra to better understand the dynamic but didn=E2=80=99t hear back.) Whatever=
 happened,
it=E2=80=99s likely typical of this sort of deal structure, and simply surp=
rising to the writers. Also, Cond=C3=A9 probably does not care about much o=
f the content, and maybe even has some disdain for it, despite the S.E.O. b=
enefits of those U.R.L.s. Of course, the pesky Nervora situation is nothing=

* Plus-size modeling in the age of Ozempic: A friend on
the body inclusivity beat texted me the other day, noting that a famously p=
lus-size model had lost quite a bit of weight in the past year, and then na=
med a couple of others who are also looking incredibly slender. =E2=80=9CI=
=E2=80=99m sort of fascinated by what happens to their careers if mid-size =
and plus-size models go full-Ozempic or even partial-Ozempic,=E2=80=9D my f=
riend remarked, referring to the folks who are microdosing GLP-1s in order =
to stealthily lose weight slowly, over time.=C2=A0

I, too,
had been thinking about this phenomenon, as I watched one of my favorite si=
ze 16 models walk a recent show, her body shrunk down to a 10. What if she =
gets really thin? Like, really really thin? Will she have the same market v=
alue?

As you can probably tell, I=E2=80=99m reluctant to name names here for a va=
riety of reasons=E2=80=94but mostly because what one chooses to do with the=
ir own body is personal. And as much as I love to joke about rich people ta=
king GLP-1s to lose 10 lbs, these drugs are
making large swaths of the population healthier, by some measure, at least.=
 That said, for these models, their body is their means of making money, an=
d my friend posed an important question: Are they bankable if they=E2=80=99=
re not plus-size, anymore?

It depends. One issue with the whole concept of body inclusivity in the mod=
eling industry is that the top plus-size models still represent unattainabl=
e beauty standards. Their waist-to-hip ratio, cleavage, and magnificent fac=
es fall in line
with what society at large deems attractive. So while they may be communica=
ting that it=E2=80=99s okay not to be skinny, they=E2=80=99re also represen=
ting the fact that it=E2=80=99s still important to be beautiful. In reality=
, the top plus-size models will work no matter what because they are incred=
ibly gorgeous=E2=80=94and, at this point, famous.=C2=A0

But the business is morphing. I reached out to some people on the talent re=
presentation side of the business to see if the rise of GLP-1s is messing w=
ith
industry dynamics. The answer is, yes, models are getting thinner (even str=
aight-size girls are on Ozempic and Mounjaro), but brands are also requesti=
ng smaller models. Anecdotally, agents are seeing more specs for models siz=
ed 6/8 or 8/10 (known as mid-size) instead of 16, 18, 20.

Why the change? Fashion brands may be projecting that the average size of t=
heir shoppers is trending downward as more people gain access to these drug=
s. But it=E2=80=99s more likely that being leaner
but not too skinny is on-trend right now. After all, nobody wants a campaig=
n star with Ozempic face.

=3D"margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;list-style-type:revert;"><li style=3D"Marg=
in:0 0 24px 0;"><em>It=E2=80=99s
like it never happened</em>: Last year, Cond=C3=A9 Nast decided to take its=
 Middle Eastern business in-house, dissolving a decade-long partnership wit=
h local outfit <a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnV=
DJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dVdEdhSlJkYTVUeTJfbEdyOTFrbz0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9w=
dWNrLm5ld3MvZ3EtY2hpbmEtanVseS1pc3N1ZS1taWdodC1nZXQtYnVyaWVkLWFtaWQtY29uZGU=
tbmFzdC1tb3Zlcy8_dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPUxpbmUrU2hlZXQrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxJT=
JGMjklMkYyNSUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUxpbmUrU2hlZXQrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslM=
jgxJTJGMjklMkYyNSUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0aW9uXHUwMDI2dXRtX3Nv=
dXJjZT1jdXN0b21lci5pb1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImludGV=
ybmFsIjoiZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6OTUzODg3fQ/f9332f03fae9cf5=
68fc5a55285a21154adb1c909f0b4dfd85100e924d4afb101" style=3D"text-decoration=
:underline;color:#d40000;">Nervora</a>, which published <em>Vogue</em> Arab=
ia under a licensing agreement. Naturally, Nervora fought back in court, cl=
aiming that Advance, Cond=C3=A9 Nast=E2=80=99s parent company, did not enga=
ge =E2=80=9Cin the good
faith negotiations for renewal of the license mandated by that license agre=
ement, but rather has only engaged in sham discussion while it plotted to u=
ndermine the protections.=E2=80=9D And yet, Cond=C3=A9 Nast proceeded with =
the breakup, earlier this month appointing <strong>Manuel Arnaut</strong>=
=E2=80=94that=E2=80=99s right, <em>no L</em>=E2=80=94as head of editorial c=
ontent at <em>Vogue </em>Arabia.=C2=A0<br/><br/>Arnaut was named editor-in-=
chief of <em>Vogue</em> Arabia in 2017, and has worked at various Cond=C3=
=A9 magazines on the
licensing partner side. =E2=80=9CJoining the parent company has always been=
 a dream of mine,=E2=80=9D he wrote in an Instagram post in early January. =
For someone like Arnaut, who=E2=80=99s been relegated to the B-team for muc=
h of his career, working with <strong>Anna Wintour </strong>is the ultimate=
 achievement. But not everyone is thrilled about the change, if only becaus=
e <em>years</em> of work have been erased from existence: Everything publis=
hed under the title <em>Vogue</em> Arabia before 2025 has been taken
offline. (Actually, all the Cond=C3=A9 brands in the Middle East=E2=80=94<e=
m>Architectural Digest</em>, <em>GQ</em>, <em>Cond=C3=A9 Nast Traveler</em>=
, too=E2=80=94have started their websites from scratch.)=C2=A0<br/><br/>My =
presumption is that Nervora owned and operated the business while simply pa=
ying a brand-licensing fee to its American partner, and therefore has no ob=
ligation to keep the articles and images online. (I reached out to Nervora =
to better understand the dynamic but didn=E2=80=99t hear back.) Whatever ha=
ppened,
it=E2=80=99s likely typical of this sort of deal structure, and simply surp=
rising to the writers. Also, Cond=C3=A9 probably does not care about much o=
f the content, and maybe even has some disdain for it, despite the S.E.O. b=
enefits of those U.R.L.s. Of course, the pesky Nervora situation is nothing=
 compared to what C.E.O. <strong>Roger Lynch</strong> is dealing with in Ch=
ina. More on that another day=E2=80=A6</li><li style=3D"Margin:0 0 24px 0;"=
><em>Plus-size modeling in the age of Ozempic</em>: A friend on
the body inclusivity beat texted me the other day, noting that a famously p=
lus-size model had lost <em>quite</em> a bit of weight in the past year, an=
d then named a couple of others who are also looking incredibly slender. =
=E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m sort of fascinated by what happens to their careers if=
 mid-size and plus-size models go full-Ozempic or even partial-Ozempic,=E2=
=80=9D my friend remarked, referring to the folks who are microdosing GLP-1=
s in order to stealthily lose weight slowly, over time.=C2=A0<br/><br/>I, t=
oo,
had been thinking about this phenomenon, as I watched one of my favorite si=
ze 16 models walk a recent show, her body shrunk down to a 10. What if she =
gets really thin? Like, really <em>really</em> thin? Will she have the same=
 market value?<br/><br/>As you can probably tell, I=E2=80=99m reluctant to =
name names here for a variety of reasons=E2=80=94but mostly because what on=
e chooses to do with their own body is personal. And as much as I love to j=
oke about rich people taking GLP-1s to lose 10 lbs, these drugs are
making large swaths of the population healthier, by some measure, at least.=
 That said, for these models, their body is their means of making money, an=
d my friend posed an important question: Are they bankable if they=E2=80=99=
re not <em>plus-size</em>, anymore?<br/><br/>It depends. One issue with the=
 whole concept of body inclusivity in the modeling industry is that the top=
 plus-size models still represent unattainable beauty standards. Their wais=
t-to-hip ratio, cleavage, and magnificent faces fall in line
with what society at large deems attractive. So while they may be communica=
ting that it=E2=80=99s okay not to be skinny, they=E2=80=99re also represen=
ting the fact that it=E2=80=99s still important to be beautiful. In reality=
, the top plus-size models will work no matter what because they are incred=
ibly gorgeous=E2=80=94and, at this point, famous.=C2=A0<br/><br/>But the bu=
siness <em>is</em> morphing. I reached out to some people on the talent rep=
resentation side of the business to see if the rise of GLP-1s is messing wi=
th
industry dynamics. The answer is, yes, models are getting thinner (even str=
aight-size girls are on Ozempic and Mounjaro), but brands are <em>also</em>=
 requesting smaller models. Anecdotally, agents are seeing more specs for m=
odels sized 6/8 or 8/10 (known as mid-size) instead of 16, 18, 20.<br/><br/=
>Why the change? Fashion brands may be projecting that the average size of =
their shoppers is trending downward as more people gain access to these dru=
gs. But it=E2=80=99s more likely that being <em>leaner</em>
but<em> not too skinny</em> is on-trend right now. After all, nobody wants =
a campaign star with Ozempic face.=C2=A0</li></ul></div>

x;">Never before has a celebrity-owned D.T.C. beauty company cracked the ma=

<em>quite</em> like <strong>Hailey Bieber</strong>=E2=80=99s Rhode. Accordi=
ng to YipitData, the 2.5-year-old brand made about <em>$90 million</em> in =

 think of another D.T.C. beauty brand that=E2=80=99s been able to generate =

gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;=

tive), top-tier talent, and <strong>Kardashian</strong>-esque marketing. Bu=
t regardless of its strength as a D.T.C. business, the path to critical mas=
s requires a major retailer to ride shotgun. For months, I=E2=80=99ve been =
hearing that Rhode was going to partner with Sephora, and a person close to=
 the retailer confirmed that the deal was <em>finally</em> official. Multip=
le
sources told me that Rhode will be available at your local Sephora later th=
is year.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"=
margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">So why did it take so long? After all, Rhode l=
aunched almost three years ago. One theory is that it was related to the =
=E2=80=9Cdrama=E2=80=9D around <strong>Selena Gomez</strong>, once a potent=
ial Mrs. Bieber herself. Rare Beauty, after all, is one of Sephora=E2=80=99=
s most important brands, and bringing in an ostensible rival could upset th=
e
partnership. =E2=80=9CIf it=E2=80=99s the right business decision, Sephora =
will navigate the drama,=E2=80=9D one person close to Sephora=E2=80=99s bus=
iness told me. But another high-level source confirmed that Gomez was <em>d=
efinitely</em> the reason for the delay, plus =E2=80=9Cgeneral reticence=E2=
=80=9D overall with respect to celebrity brands. =E2=80=9CIt took them clos=
e to a decade to bring in <strong>Ariana Grande</strong>=E2=80=99s fragranc=
e,=E2=80=9D this source noted, adding that although Gomez=E2=80=99s Rare Be=
auty is =E2=80=9Cgreat,=E2=80=9D the business is still maturing. =E2=80=9CT=
hey
need new hits constantly.=E2=80=9D (Sephora did not respond to a request fo=
r comment.)</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin:0;">Of course, the partnership with Sephora=E2=80=94which curren=
tly commands 30 percent of the U.S. beauty retail market=E2=80=94has huge i=
mplications for Bieber=E2=80=99s business. Sephora distribution will cannib=
alize Rhode=E2=80=99s e-commerce site, which is the inevitable downside whe=
n strong D.T.C. brands expand into offline retail. But the exposure to new =
customers, and the
opportunity to get in front of the millions of people who only shop for bea=
uty I.R.L., is invaluable. At the very least, it will recenter the years-lo=
ng rivalry between Bieber and Gomez as they fight for floor space and Gen Z=
 wallet share. <br/><br/>Rhode and Rare will be in more direct competition =
than ever, especially since the former has pushed into color. (Rhode alread=
y has tinted gloss, blush, and lip liners, and I=E2=80=99ve heard that bron=
zer and other makeup is on the horizon.) And while it will
almost certainly never happen, it would be cool if the two brands collabora=
ted on a Rare x Rhode Soft Touch Pocket Blush.</p></div>

x;">Meanwhile, I=E2=80=99m still amazed at how seamlessly Rhode is morphing=

ay it=E2=80=99s shot, to the lighting, to the monochromatic color palette, =
and even to its star: <strong>Tate McRae</strong>, the 21-year-old pop sing=
er
<em>Vogue</em> just anointed a <em>fashion icon</em>. Yes, it=E2=80=99s not=
 the first time another famous face has appeared in Rhode=E2=80=99s ads=E2=
=80=93=E2=80=93<strong>Claudia Schiffer</strong> and <strong>Matilda Djerf<=
/strong> collaborated with Bieber last year=E2=80=93=E2=80=93but McRae is t=
he most <em>au courant</em>, and proof that Skims isn=E2=80=99t the only br=
and in touch with the zeitgeist.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-botto=
m:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">That said, the m=
imicry makes sense. I hear that
Grede and <strong>Michael D. Ratner</strong>, Hailey=E2=80=99s business par=
tner and a producer on the <em>Justin Bieber: Seasons</em> docuseries, are =
pals. Also, Grede & Co.=E2=80=99s genius has an outsize, unavoidable in=
fluence beyond beauty. But you have to wonder if the Grede-Bieber connectio=
n is privately grating to Kim and the other Skims stakeholders, particularl=
y as Rhode adopts more and more of the Skims brand playbook. My understandi=
ng is that it=E2=80=99s not=E2=80=94or not yet, anyway. Rhode plays in an e=
ntirely
different category, and so the two brands aren=E2=80=99t competing for mark=
et share.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D=
"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">I=E2=80=99ve already <a href=3D"https://email=

A lot of the brand=E2=80=99s success comes down to <em>how </em>it messages=

iscerning insiders I know praised Bieber=E2=80=99s ability to make
=E2=80=9Cpeptides=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Clip peptide treatments=E2=80=9D a <=

<p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;"><em><br/></em>Among other things, =
the strategy seems to concede that
everything already exists, and that simply repackaging an established produ=

 A retail pro I know suggested that not calling it a =E2=80=9Cliner=E2=80=

 but that the messaging upside seems to outweigh the risks. That said, they
did just make $90 million in two months online, so <em>someone</em> must be=
 searching for =E2=80=9Cpeptide.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bo=

=80=99t even think of calling Peptide Lip Shape a <em>liner</em>. A person =

ck unpleasantly migrates

=99s version is meant to be used as a =E2=80=9Cshaper=E2=80=9D to make lips=
 more full. It=E2=80=99s also dual-ended and has a little silicone smudger =
to =E2=80=9Cdiffuse=E2=80=9D color or =E2=80=9Ctidy up the edges.=E2=80=9D =
Rhode=E2=80=99s <em>really</em> sticking to this spiel, and I love it, but =
to be clear: This product=E2=80=99s main function is lip lining.</p></div>
